interview_name: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan

main_summary.summary: Joint interview conducted on September 19, 2011 by Joe Mosnier at the Highlander Research and Education Center in New Market, Tennessee, with Candie Anderson Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan, married musicians and educators central to the folk-music substrate of the southern civil rights movement. Candie, raised in California and a Unitarian household, traveled to Nashville on the Fisk Exchange Program in January 1960 and participated in the Nashville sit-ins under Reverend James Lawson's non-violent training. Guy had come to Highlander Folk School in 1953 on Pete Seeger's recommendation after studying folklore at UCLA under Wayland D. Hand and absorbing the Lomaxes' Library of Congress recordings. Together they describe Highlander's April 1960 gathering of sit-in students that helped seed SNCC, where Guy taught "We Shall Overcome," "Keep Your Eyes on the Prize," and "I'm Gonna Sit at the Welcome Table." Candie narrates Guy's transition from teaching songs to documenting movement music with an Ampex tape recorder, his collaboration with Moe Asch at Folkways Records and Sing Out! magazine, his work with Septima Clark and Myles Horton, and the Highlander pivot toward Appalachian organizing after 1965 that eventually drew the couple to Pitzer College in California.
key_themes: We Shall Overcome / freedom song tradition
main_summary.historical_significance:
Provides first-person testimony from the two figures most responsible for transmitting the freedom-song repertoire across Movement communities. The interview documents the specific April 1960 Highlander gathering at which Guy Carawan taught "We Shall Overcome" to the sit-in student delegations whose subsequent SNCC founding meeting carried the song into broader circulation. Candie identifies Jamila Jones as the Montgomery teenager who improvised the "We Are Not Afraid" verse during the 1959 Highlander raid, an attribution that anchors a specific origin claim for one of the movement's most-sung verses.

The interview also captures Guy's deliberate 1961 pivot from song-leading to field recording with an Ampex tape recorder distributed through Moe Asch's Folkways Records and Sing Out!/Oak Publications, establishing the documentary record now held at the Library of Congress. Candie's account of Highlander's mid-1960s shift toward Appalachian organizing in response to SNCC's challenge to white movement participants documents how the school adapted its constituency under Myles Horton's leadership.

chapters.title: Meeting at Highlander, April 1960
chapters.summary: Candie describes the April 1, 1960 Highlander gathering at Monteagle, Tennessee, where sit-in students convened weeks after Greensboro. Guy taught freedom songs including "I'm Gonna Sit at the Welcome Table," "Keep Your Eyes on the Prize," and "We Shall Overcome." Myles Horton led breakout discussions on non-violence as tactic versus philosophy and on jail-no-bail strategy. James Bevel and Bernard Lafayette participated in the singing breakouts when sessions grew tense.

chapters.title: Septima Clark and Myles Horton
chapters.summary: Guy recalls driving Septima Clark on Highlander business in the late 1950s, including instances when police stopped them because Clark sat in the front seat. Candie describes Clark as a sensible neighbor on Johns Island, South Carolina, and emphasizes her toughness against the assumption of a sweet demeanor. Both characterize Myles Horton's educational method as drawing on participants' own experiences without intimidation.

chapters.title: Pomona to Fisk Exchange
chapters.summary: Candie describes her California upbringing in a Unitarian fellowship, her interest awakened by Montgomery bus boycott coverage and high-school study of Gandhi, and her path to Pomona College where a returning student introduced her to the Fisk Exchange Program. She arrived in Nashville in January 1960, timed to coincide with the sit-in mobilization that semester.

chapters.title: Nashville Sit-ins and Mass Meeting Music
chapters.summary: Candie recalls non-violent training under Reverend James Lawson at Fisk University and the electric reaction when Greensboro broke. She contrasts the camp songs and rock-and-roll she knew with the community singing of Nashville mass meetings, where religious songs were adapted with a few changed words into freedom songs. The Nashville Quartet became important to this work.

chapters.title: Guy's Musical Formation
chapters.summary: Candie traces Guy's California background: people-songs work in Los Angeles, the Oliver family who introduced him to political music, his master's in sociology at UCLA where he absorbed Library of Congress recordings and the Lomaxes, and folklorist Wayland D. Hand's warning to let folk music remain politically neutral. After meeting Pete Seeger, Guy traveled the South in summer 1953 with Rambling Jack Elliott and Frank Hamilton, ending at Highlander.

chapters.title: Traveling Movement Concerts
chapters.summary: The Carawans married in 1961. Candie describes their travel to civil rights communities across the South, including a Clarksdale, Mississippi joint concert with opera student Mary Jane Pigee and her mother Vera Mae Pigee's NAACP youth organizing. Police escorted them out of the black part of Clarksdale and ultimately jailed them. She was pregnant at the time.

chapters.title: Sung Verses and Jamila Jones
chapters.summary: Candie and Guy perform verses of "Keep Your Eyes on the Prize" and "We Shall Overcome." Candie identifies Jamila Jones, a thirteen-year-old in an adult Montgomery delegation, as the person who first hummed and inserted the "We Are Not Afraid" verse during the 1959 raid on Highlander, in the dark with police inside the building.
keywords: Keep Your Eyes on the Prize

chapters.title: Documenting Movement Music
chapters.summary: Candie describes Guy's 1961 decision after the Freedom Rider release in Jackson, Mississippi (following imprisonment at Parchman) to shift from song-leading to documentation. He purchased an Ampex tape recorder and built a relationship with Moe Asch at Folkways Records and Irwin Silber at Sing Out! magazine. The first Oak Publications songbook of freedom songs followed. Guy was stabbed by police at an SCLC annual conference and had his mimeographed materials confiscated.

chapters.title: Music as Non-Violent Tool
chapters.summary: Candie recounts John Lewis's line that the movement without its music would be a bird without wings. She describes the We Shall Overcome Fund's distribution of commercial royalties to southern communities and quotes Bernice Johnson Reagon on the aggressive, non-passive power of movement singing. She discusses the shift from "We Shall Overcome" to "We'll Never Turn Back" when the original came to feel too optimistic or co-opted.

chapters.title: Tree of Life and Ed Cabbell
chapters.summary: Candie and Guy perform "Ain't You Got a Right to the Tree of Life," a Johns Island song they collected. Candie tells the story of Ed Cabbell, a West Virginia African American friend and historian of Appalachian Black communities, who attended an early Highlander workshop. The performances bracket Candie's reflection on carrying fear in retrospect that she did not feel at the time.

chapters.title: Highlander Shifts to Appalachia
chapters.summary: Candie describes Highlander's 1965 pivot in response to SNCC's challenge to white participants. Myles Horton accepted that Black people led the civil rights movement and that white organizers should build allies in white communities. Septima Clark moved to SCLC's Citizenship Education Program; Bernice Robinson followed. Highlander brought Appalachian staff onboard and began listening tours in mountain communities, looking for places with both anger and hope.

chapters.title: Pitzer College and Field Study
chapters.summary: After two years in Pittsfield, New York, the Carawans took a Highlander assignment in Pike County, Kentucky. A Pitzer College political-science faculty member named Lucien recruited Guy to teach there. The family moved to Claremont, California for their son's kindergarten year. After one semester Guy created an Appalachian Field Study Program, taking Pitzer and Pomona College students into the mountains. Candie performs Joan Boyd's "Come Home," a song from Big Stone Gap, Virginia.

loc_video.caption: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in New Market, Tennessee, 2011 September 19
